fact: There are three writing systems in Japanese: Hiragana, Katakana, and Kanji.
Hiragana has 46 basic characters.
Katakana is primarily used for foreign words and names.
Kanji characters originally came from China.
Japanese nouns do not change form for singular or plural.
The Japanese language has particles that show grammatical relationships.
In Japan it is polite to bow when greeting someone.
Japanese people remove their shoes before entering a home.
The Japanese yen (¥) is the currency of Japan.
Tokyo is the capital of Japan.
Japan is made up of over 6,800 islands.
The cherry blossom (sakura) is a beloved symbol of spring.
Japan has one of the world’s highest life expectancies.
Japanese trains are known for being punctual.
Origami is the traditional art of paper folding.
The Japanese tea ceremony (茶道, sadō) is a cultural tradition.
Mount Fuji is the highest mountain in Japan.
Japanese school uniforms are widely used.
Anime and manga are popular forms of entertainment in Japan.
Japan has a national sport called sumo wrestling.
Etiquette for eating includes not sticking chopsticks upright in rice.
In Japan tipping is not customary and can be considered rude.
The Shinkansen (bullet train) can travel over 300 km/h.
Japanese convenience stores (konbini) are very efficient and varied.
Many Japanese homes use futons instead of beds.
In Japan birthdays are not traditionally celebrated as widely as in the West.
Japanese people often say 'itadakimasu' before eating a meal.
Sushi originated in Japan and is a popular traditional dish.
Japan has four distinct seasons.
The Japanese flag is a simple red circle on a white background.
Japan has more than 200 volcanoes.
In Japanese, long vowels change the meaning of words.
Kanji characters can have multiple readings depending on context.
Japanese trains have priority seating for elderly and disabled passengers.
Seasonal greetings are common in Japanese communication.
Japan has one of the lowest crime rates in the world.
The tradition of hanami involves picnicking under cherry blossoms.
Many words in Japanese come from English but are adapted in pronunciation.
Japan has a strong vending machine culture with machines everywhere.
Etiquette includes bowing instead of shaking hands.
Japanese calligraphy (書道, shodō) is a respected art form.
Gift-giving is an important part of Japanese culture.
The Japanese language uses honorifics to show respect.
Japanese restaurants often display plastic food models in their windows.
The Japanese calendar includes many national holidays.
Classical poetry forms like haiku are part of Japanese literary history.
Japanese gardens emphasize harmony, simplicity, and nature.
Many Japanese companies offer lifetime employment.
Public baths (銭湯, sentō) and hot springs (温泉, onsen) are popular in Japan.
